Retrieved Documents: 32 documents

Based on the review of the previously mentioned reasons supporting "unretentive" as the correct option (\( \boxed{\text{D}}\)), along with additional insights gathered about denture stability concerns and mechanisms affecting initial wear, I now believe there remains strong basis confirming my earlier stance. The literature underscores key points reinforcing why improper fit or insufficient retentive means would indeed rapidly induce instability in newly fitted dentures almost immediately following placement — aligning perfectly with what we observe clinically for this scenario. Specifically,
* Poorly fitting/designed prosthetics inherently struggle to securely bond with surrounding soft/hard tissues given inadequate physical contact/surface adhesion necessary for lasting stabilization;
* Rapid adaptation isn't typically seen so swiftly unless fundamental flaws exist rendering denture security compromised from start rather than evolving slowly over days/months per usual physiological responses driving eventual equilibrium adjustments;
Thus, continuing agreement stands validated even stronger amidst fresh evidential backing highlighting transient nature of unacceptably insecure appliances vs longer maturation periods required before fully comfortable/unproblematic states emerge consistently observed clinically post-adjustment.
